31	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
32	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
33	
34	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
35	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
36	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
37	equivalently (and functorially) the set
38	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
39	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
40	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
41	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
42	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
43	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
44	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
45	
46	We first record the local-algebra core of the "tangent space via dual
47	numbers" identification: for a local \(k\)-algebra \(R\) with maximal
48	ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field \(\kappa\), and with the
49	\(k\)-rational-point hypothesis that the structure map
50	\(k \to \kappa\) is an isomorphism, the \(k\)-derivations of \(R\)
51	into \(\kappa\), the local \(k\)-algebra maps
52	\(R \to k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\), and the linear functionals
53	on the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2\) all agree.
54	Applied to the local ring of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) at the identity, this is
55	the identification \(\tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k} \cong
56	\Hom(\mathfrak m_0/\mathfrak m_0^2, k)\) used in the main theorem.
57	
58	\begin{lemma}[Derivations descend to the cotangent space]\leanok
59	  \label{lem:tangent_derivation_descends}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.derivationCotangentDual}
61	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a local \(k\)-algebra with maximal
62	  ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field \(\kappa\). Every
63	  \(k\)-derivation \(d : R \to \kappa\) vanishes on
64	  \(\mathfrak m^2\), and its restriction to \(\mathfrak m\) is
65	  \(R\)-linear; consequently \(d\) induces an \(R\)-linear map
66	  \[
67	    \bar d : \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \longrightarrow \kappa .
68	  \]
69	\end{lemma}
70	
71	\begin{proof}\leanok
72	  Every element of \(\mathfrak m\) acts by zero on the \(R\)-module
73	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\), since the action factors through the
74	  residue class. For \(x, y \in \mathfrak m\) the Leibniz rule gives
75	  \(d(xy) = x \cdot d(y) + y \cdot d(x) = 0\), so \(d\) vanishes on
76	  \(\mathfrak m^2\). For \(r \in R\) and \(x \in \mathfrak m\),
77	  \(d(rx) = r \cdot d(x) + x \cdot d(r) = r \cdot d(x)\), so
78	  \(d|_{\mathfrak m}\) is \(R\)-linear; it therefore descends to the
79	  quotient \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2\).
80	\end{proof}
81	
82	\begin{lemma}[Derivations are the cotangent dual]\leanok
83	  \label{lem:tangent_derivation_cotangent_dual}
84	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.derivationEquivCotangentDual}
85	  \uses{lem:tangent_derivation_descends}
86	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a local \(k\)-algebra whose
87	  structure map \(k \to \kappa\) to the residue field is an
88	  isomorphism. Then descent \(d \mapsto \bar d\)
89	  (\cref{lem:tangent_derivation_descends}) is an isomorphism of
90	  abelian groups
91	  \[
92	    \operatorname{Der}_k(R, \kappa)
93	    \;\cong\;
94	    \Hom_R(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2, \kappa).
95	  \]
96	\end{lemma}
97	
98	\begin{proof}\leanok
99	  Write \(\sigma : \kappa \to k\) for the inverse of the structure
100	  map. Each \(r \in R\) splits canonically as
101	  \(r = c_r + x_r\) with constant part
102	  \(c_r := \sigma(\bar r) \cdot 1 \in k \cdot 1\) and
103	  \(x_r := r - c_r \in \mathfrak m\) (its residue class vanishes by
104	  construction).
105	
106	  \emph{Injectivity.} A \(k\)-derivation kills the constants
107	  \(k \cdot 1\), so \(d(r) = d(x_r)\) for every \(r\): the derivation
108	  is determined by its restriction to \(\mathfrak m\), i.e.\ by
109	  \(\bar d\).
110	
111	  \emph{Surjectivity.} Given an \(R\)-linear
112	  \(\varphi : \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \to \kappa\), set
113	  \(d(r) := \varphi(x_r \bmod \mathfrak m^2)\). Additivity and
114	  \(k\)-linearity follow since \(r \mapsto c_r\) is \(k\)-linear. For
115	  the Leibniz rule, expand
116	  \[
117	    x_{rs} \;=\; c_r\, x_s + c_s\, x_r + x_r x_s ,
118	  \]
119	  where \(x_r x_s \in \mathfrak m^2\) dies in the cotangent space;
120	  since \(r\) and \(c_r\) have the same residue class, they act
121	  identically on \(\kappa\), whence
122	  \(d(rs) = c_r \varphi(\bar x_s) + c_s \varphi(\bar x_r)
123	    = r \cdot d(s) + s \cdot d(r)\).
124	  On elements of \(\mathfrak m\) the constant part vanishes, so the
125	  two constructions are mutually inverse.
126	\end{proof}
127	
128	\begin{lemma}[Dual-number points are derivations]\leanok
129	  \label{lem:tangent_dual_number_derivation}
130	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.localDualNumberHomEquivDerivation}
131	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a local \(k\)-algebra whose
132	  structure map \(k \to \kappa\) is an isomorphism. Sending a
133	  \(k\)-algebra homomorphism \(f : R \to k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\)
134	  to the \(\varepsilon\)-coefficient of its values is a bijection
135	  \[
136	    \{\, f : R \to k_\varepsilon \ \text{local $k$-algebra maps} \,\}
137	    \;\cong\;
138	    \operatorname{Der}_k(R, \kappa),
139	  \]
140	  where locality means \(f(\mathfrak m) \subseteq (\varepsilon)\),
141	  the maximal ideal of the local ring \(k_\varepsilon\).
142	\end{lemma}
143	
144	\begin{proof}\leanok
145	  Write \(f(r) = a(r) + b(r)\varepsilon\). Splitting
146	  \(r = c_r + x_r\) into constant part and maximal-ideal part as in
147	  \cref{lem:tangent_derivation_cotangent_dual}, locality and
148	  \(k\)-linearity force \(a(r) = a(c_r) = \sigma(\bar r)\): the
149	  constant component of \(f\) is the residue map read through
150	  \(\kappa \cong k\). Multiplicativity of \(f\) and
151	  \(\varepsilon^2 = 0\) give
152	  \(b(rs) = a(r)\,b(s) + a(s)\,b(r)\), which — transported through
153	  \(\kappa \cong k\) — is exactly the Leibniz rule for the map
154	  \(D := \bar b : R \to \kappa\); \(k\)-linearity of \(D\) is
155	  inherited from \(f\). Conversely, a derivation
156	  \(D : R \to \kappa\) assembles with the residue map into
157	  \(f(r) := \sigma(\bar r) + \sigma(D(r))\,\varepsilon\), a local
158	  \(k\)-algebra homomorphism by the same computation. The two
159	  constructions are mutually inverse: the constant component of any
160	  local \(f\) is forced as above, and the \(\varepsilon\)-component
161	  is preserved by both directions.
162	\end{proof}
163	
164	\begin{corollary}[The tangent space is the cotangent dual]\leanok
165	  \label{cor:tangent_dual_number_cotangent_dual}
166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.localDualNumberHomEquivCotangentDual}
167	  \uses{lem:tangent_derivation_cotangent_dual,
168	        lem:tangent_dual_number_derivation}
169	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a local \(k\)-algebra whose
170	  structure map \(k \to \kappa\) is an isomorphism. Then the set of
171	  local \(k\)-algebra maps \(R \to k_\varepsilon\) — the dual-number
172	  points of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) lifting the closed point — is
173	  in bijection with
174	  \(\Hom_R(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2, \kappa)\), the dual of the
175	  cotangent space.
176	\end{corollary}
177	
178	\begin{proof}\leanok
179	  Compose the bijections of
180	  \cref{lem:tangent_dual_number_derivation} and
181	  \cref{lem:tangent_derivation_cotangent_dual}.
182	\end{proof}
183	
184	\begin{lemma}[The cotangent dual is $\kappa$-linear]\leanok
185	  \label{lem:tangent_cotangent_dual_kappa_linear}
186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cotangentDualExtendScalars}
187	  Let \(R\) be a local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and
188	  residue field \(\kappa\). Every \(R\)-linear map
189	  \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \to \kappa\) is \(\kappa\)-linear for
190	  the natural \(\kappa\)-vector-space structures on both sides:
191	  \[
192	    \Hom_R(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2, \kappa)
193	    \;=\;
194	    \Hom_\kappa(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2, \kappa)
195	    \;=\; (\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2)^{\vee}.
196	  \]
197	\end{lemma}
198	
199	\begin{proof}\leanok
200	  Both \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2\) and \(\kappa\) are modules over
201	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\), and their \(R\)-actions factor through
202	  the surjection \(R \twoheadrightarrow \kappa\). Hence for an
203	  \(R\)-linear \(\varphi\) and a scalar \(c = \bar r \in \kappa\),
204	  \(\varphi(c \cdot x) = \varphi(r \cdot x) = r \cdot \varphi(x)
205	    = c \cdot \varphi(x)\).
206	\end{proof}
207	
208	\begin{corollary}[Tangent space as the dual vector space of
209	  $\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2$]\leanok
210	  \label{cor:tangent_dual_number_cotangent_space_dual}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.localDualNumberHomEquivCotangentSpaceDual}
212	  \uses{cor:tangent_dual_number_cotangent_dual,
213	        lem:tangent_cotangent_dual_kappa_linear}
214	  In the situation of \cref{cor:tangent_dual_number_cotangent_dual},
215	  the dual-number points of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) lifting the
216	  closed point are in bijection with the \(\kappa\)-linear dual
217	  vector space \((\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2)^{\vee}\). In particular
218	  their \(\kappa\)-dimension equals
219	  \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2\) when the latter is
220	  finite.
221	\end{corollary}
222	
223	\begin{proof}\leanok
224	  Compose \cref{cor:tangent_dual_number_cotangent_dual} with the
225	  identification of \cref{lem:tangent_cotangent_dual_kappa_linear};
226	  the dimension statement is the equality
227	  \(\dim_\kappa V^{\vee} = \dim_\kappa V\) for finite-dimensional
228	  \(V\).
229	\end{proof}
230	
231	The local-algebra bijections above consume local homomorphisms out of
232	the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\). The next three statements supply the
233	geometric input: morphisms \(\operatorname{Spec} k_\varepsilon \to X\)
234	landing at a fixed point \(x\) are exactly such stalk data.
235	
236	\begin{lemma}[Local maps to the dual numbers]\leanok
237	  \label{lem:tangent_dual_number_local_iff}
238	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalHom_dualNumber_iff}
239	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(R\) a local ring, and
240	  \(f : R \to k_\varepsilon = k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) a ring
241	  homomorphism. Then \(f\) is a local homomorphism if and only if the
242	  constant component of \(f\) kills the maximal ideal:
243	  \[
244	    f(\mathfrak m) \subseteq (\varepsilon)
245	    \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
246	    \forall\, x \in \mathfrak m,\ \operatorname{fst}(f(x)) = 0 .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}\leanok
251	  The dual numbers \(k_\varepsilon\) form a local ring whose maximal
252	  ideal is \((\varepsilon)\), the kernel of the constant-component
253	  projection \(\operatorname{fst}\); an element of
254	  \(k_\varepsilon\) is a unit iff its constant component is a nonzero
255	  element of the field \(k\). If \(f\) is local and
256	  \(x \in \mathfrak m\), then \(x\) is a nonunit, so \(f(x)\) is a
257	  nonunit, i.e.\ \(\operatorname{fst}(f(x)) = 0\). Conversely, if the
258	  constant component kills \(\mathfrak m\) and \(f(a)\) is a unit,
259	  then \(\operatorname{fst}(f(a)) \neq 0\), so \(a \notin \mathfrak m\),
260	  i.e.\ \(a\) is a unit.
261	\end{proof}
262	
263	\begin{lemma}[$\operatorname{Spec}$ of a local ring at a fixed point]\leanok
264	  \label{lem:tangent_spec_local_ring_at}
265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.specToEquivOfLocalRingAt}
266	  Let \(R\) be a local ring, \(X\) a scheme, and \(x \in X\) a fixed
267	  point. Morphisms \(\operatorname{Spec} R \to X\) carrying the closed
268	  point of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) to \(x\) are in bijection with the
269	  local ring homomorphisms \(\mathcal O_{X,x} \to R\):
270	  \[
271	    \{\, f : \operatorname{Spec} R \to X \mid f(\mathfrak m_R) = x \,\}
272	    \;\cong\;
273	    \{\, \varphi : \mathcal O_{X,x} \to R \ \text{local} \,\}.
274	  \]
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{proof}\leanok
278	  This is the fiber at \(x\) of the standard description of morphisms
279	  out of the spectrum of a local ring (Stacks 01J6; in Mathlib,
280	  \texttt{SpecToEquivOfLocalRing}):
281	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Spec} R, X)\) is the set of pairs
282	  \((y, \varphi)\) of a point \(y \in X\) and a local homomorphism
283	  \(\varphi : \mathcal O_{X,y} \to R\). Forward, a morphism \(f\) with
284	  \(f(\mathfrak m_R) = x\) induces on stalks a local homomorphism
285	  \(\mathcal O_{X,f(\mathfrak m_R)} \to \mathcal O_{\operatorname{Spec} R,\mathfrak m_R}
286	    = R\), transported to \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) along
287	  \(f(\mathfrak m_R) = x\). Backward, a local \(\varphi\) yields
288	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(\varphi)\) composed with the canonical map
289	  \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal O_{X,x} \to X\), which carries the
290	  closed point to \(x\) precisely because \(\varphi\) is local. The
291	  two constructions are mutually inverse by the same computation as
292	  for the unfibered bijection, the transport along \(f(\mathfrak m_R) = x\)
293	  cancelling on both sides.
294	\end{proof}
295	
296	\begin{corollary}[Dual-number points at a fixed point]\leanok
297	  \label{cor:tangent_spec_dual_number_at}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.specDualNumberAtEquiv}
299	  \uses{lem:tangent_dual_number_local_iff,
300	        lem:tangent_spec_local_ring_at}
301	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) a scheme, and \(x \in X\). The
302	  dual-number points of \(X\) at \(x\) — morphisms
303	  \(\operatorname{Spec} k_\varepsilon \to X\) carrying the closed point
304	  to \(x\) — are in bijection with the ring homomorphisms
305	  \(\mathcal O_{X,x} \to k_\varepsilon\) whose constant component kills
306	  the maximal ideal. This is exactly the interface consumed by
307	  \cref{cor:tangent_dual_number_cotangent_space_dual}, once the
308	  \(k\)-algebra structure on the stalk at a \(k\)-rational point of a
309	  \(k\)-scheme is threaded through.
310	\end{corollary}
311	
312	\begin{proof}\leanok
313	  Instantiate \cref{lem:tangent_spec_local_ring_at} at
314	  \(R = k_\varepsilon\) (a local ring, its maximal ideal being
315	  \((\varepsilon)\)) and rewrite the locality condition by
316	  \cref{lem:tangent_dual_number_local_iff}.
317	\end{proof}
318	
319	The stalk data above is so far only ring-theoretic. The structure
320	morphism of a \(k\)-scheme endows each stalk with a canonical
321	\(k\)-algebra structure, and morphisms \emph{over} \(\operatorname{Spec} k\)
322	are exactly those whose stalk data is \(k\)-linear. This is the missing
323	thread between \cref{cor:tangent_spec_dual_number_at} and
324	\cref{cor:tangent_dual_number_cotangent_space_dual}.
325	
326	\begin{definition}[Structure homomorphism into a stalk]\leanok
327	  \label{def:tangent_stalk_structure_hom}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.stalkStructureHom}
329	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) a scheme, \(f : X \to \operatorname{Spec} k\)
330	  a morphism, and \(x \in X\). The \emph{structure homomorphism}
331	  \(\sigma_x : k \to \mathcal O_{X,x}\) is the composite
332	  \[
333	    k \;\cong\; \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} k, \mathcal O)
334	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_{\operatorname{Spec} k,\, f(x)}
335	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_{X,x}
336	  \]
337	  of the global-sections identification, the germ map at \(f(x)\), and
338	  the stalk map of \(f\) at \(x\).
339	\end{definition}
340	
341	\begin{definition}[The stalk as a $k$-algebra]\leanok
342	  \label{def:tangent_stalk_algebra}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.stalkAlgebra}
344	  \uses{def:tangent_stalk_structure_hom}
345	  In the situation of \cref{def:tangent_stalk_structure_hom}, the ring
346	  homomorphism \(\sigma_x\) makes the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) a
347	  \(k\)-algebra, with \(\operatorname{algebraMap} = \sigma_x\).
348	\end{definition}
349	
350	\begin{lemma}[Canonical map from the spectrum of a stalk, over $\operatorname{Spec} k$]\leanok
351	  \label{lem:tangent_from_spec_stalk_over}
352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromSpecStalk_comp_eq}
353	  \uses{def:tangent_stalk_structure_hom}
354	  In the situation of \cref{def:tangent_stalk_structure_hom}, the
355	  canonical morphism
356	  \(c_x : \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal O_{X,x} \to X\) followed by \(f\)
357	  is the morphism of affine schemes induced by the structure
358	  homomorphism:
359	  \[
360	    f \circ c_x \;=\; \operatorname{Spec}(\sigma_x).
361	  \]
362	\end{lemma}
363	
364	\begin{proof}\leanok
365	  By naturality of the canonical map out of the spectrum of a stalk,
366	  \(f \circ c_x\) factors as
367	  \(c_{f(x)} \circ \operatorname{Spec}(f^\sharp_x)\), where
368	  \(f^\sharp_x : \mathcal O_{\operatorname{Spec} k, f(x)} \to \mathcal O_{X,x}\)
369	  is the stalk map of \(f\) and
370	  \(c_{f(x)} : \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal O_{\operatorname{Spec} k, f(x)}
371	  \to \operatorname{Spec} k\) is the canonical map for the affine scheme
372	  \(\operatorname{Spec} k\). The latter is
373	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\) applied to the composite of the
374	  global-sections identification
375	  \(k \cong \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} k, \mathcal O)\) with the germ
376	  map at \(f(x)\). By contravariant functoriality of
377	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\), the composite is
378	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\) of
379	  \(f^\sharp_x \circ (\text{germ at } f(x)) \circ (\text{identification})
380	  = \sigma_x\).
381	\end{proof}
382	
383	\begin{lemma}[Over-triangles are structure-compatibilities]\leanok
384	  \label{lem:tangent_over_triangle_iff}
385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.comp_eq_spec_iff}
386	  \uses{def:tangent_stalk_structure_hom}
387	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(f : X \to \operatorname{Spec} k\) a morphism
388	  of schemes, \(S\) a local ring, \(g : \operatorname{Spec} S \to X\),
389	  and \(\psi : k \to S\) a ring homomorphism. Write
390	  \(x = g(\mathfrak m_S)\) for the image of the closed point and
391	  \(\varphi_g : \mathcal O_{X,x} \to S\) for the local homomorphism
392	  induced by \(g\) on stalks
393	  (\cref{lem:tangent_spec_local_ring_at}). Then
394	  \[
395	    f \circ g = \operatorname{Spec}(\psi)
396	    \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
397	    \varphi_g \circ \sigma_x = \psi .
398	  \]
399	\end{lemma}
400	
401	\begin{proof}\leanok
402	  \uses{lem:tangent_from_spec_stalk_over, lem:tangent_spec_local_ring_at}
403	  By \cref{lem:tangent_spec_local_ring_at} the morphism \(g\) factors
404	  as \(g = c_x \circ \operatorname{Spec}(\varphi_g)\) with
405	  \(c_x : \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal O_{X,x} \to X\) the canonical
406	  map. Hence, using \cref{lem:tangent_from_spec_stalk_over} and
407	  contravariant functoriality,
408	  \[
409	    f \circ g
410	      = (f \circ c_x) \circ \operatorname{Spec}(\varphi_g)
411	      = \operatorname{Spec}(\sigma_x) \circ \operatorname{Spec}(\varphi_g)
412	      = \operatorname{Spec}(\varphi_g \circ \sigma_x).
413	  \]
414	  Since \(\operatorname{Spec}\) is faithful on ring homomorphisms (it
415	  is fully faithful onto affine schemes), the equality
416	  \(f \circ g = \operatorname{Spec}(\psi)\) holds if and only if
417	  \(\varphi_g \circ \sigma_x = \psi\).
418	\end{proof}
419	
420	\begin{corollary}[Over-$\operatorname{Spec} k$ dual-number points are local $k$-algebra maps]\leanok
421	  \label{cor:tangent_over_dual_number_alghom}
422	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overDualNumberAtEquivAlgHom}
423	  \uses{def:tangent_stalk_algebra,
424	        cor:tangent_spec_dual_number_at,
425	        lem:tangent_over_triangle_iff}
426	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec} k\),
427	  and \(x \in X\). The dual-number points of \(X\) at \(x\) \emph{over}
428	  \(\operatorname{Spec} k\) — morphisms
429	  \(g : \operatorname{Spec} k_\varepsilon \to X\) over
430	  \(\operatorname{Spec} k\) carrying the closed point to \(x\) — are in
431	  bijection with the \(k\)-\emph{algebra} homomorphisms
432	  \(\mathcal O_{X,x} \to k_\varepsilon\) whose constant component kills
433	  the maximal ideal. (A plain ring homomorphism

509	\begin{lemma}[The image of a section is a rational point]\leanok
510	  \label{lem:tangent_section_rational}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.bijective_algebraMap_residueField_of_section}
512	  \uses{def:tangent_stalk_algebra}
513	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec} k\),
514	  \(e : \operatorname{Spec} k \to X\) a section of the structure
515	  morphism, and \(x = e(*)\). Then \(x\) is a \(k\)-rational point:
516	  the structure map \(k \to \kappa(x)\) to the residue field is
517	  bijective.
518	\end{lemma}
519	
520	\begin{proof}\leanok
521	  \uses{lem:tangent_section_retraction}
522	  Injectivity is automatic: a ring homomorphism out of a field into a
523	  nonzero ring is injective. For surjectivity, the retraction
524	  \(\varphi_e : \mathcal O_{X,x} \to k\) of
525	  \cref{lem:tangent_section_retraction} is a local homomorphism into
526	  a field, so it kills the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m_x\) and
527	  descends to a field homomorphism
528	  \(\bar\varphi : \kappa(x) \to k\) with
529	  \(\bar\varphi \circ \iota = \operatorname{id}_k\), where
530	  \(\iota : k \to \kappa(x)\) is the structure map. Given
531	  \(a \in \kappa(x)\), the element
532	  \(a - \iota(\bar\varphi(a))\) lies in the kernel of
533	  \(\bar\varphi\); but \(\bar\varphi\) is a homomorphism of fields,
534	  hence injective, so \(a = \iota(\bar\varphi(a))\). Thus \(\iota\)
535	  is surjective.
536	\end{proof}

694	% exponential sequence of sheaves of Abelian groups:
695	%    \(\)0\to \mc O_{\!X} \to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^* \to \mc O_{\!X}^* \to 1,\(\)

707	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
708	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
709	        lem:tangent_derivation_cotangent_dual,

711	        cor:tangent_spec_dual_number_at,

713	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~5.11. By
714	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
715	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
716	  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
717	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
718	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
719	  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
720	  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.
721	
722	  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
723	  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
724	  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
725	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
726	  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
727	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
728	  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\): such points are, by
729	  \cref{cor:tangent_spec_dual_number_at}, the local
730	  \(k\)-algebra maps \(\mathcal O_{P,e} \to k_\varepsilon\), which by
731	  \cref{cor:tangent_dual_number_cotangent_dual} (via
732	  \cref{lem:tangent_derivation_cotangent_dual}) form the dual of the
733	  cotangent space

735	  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
736	  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
737	  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
738	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
739	  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
740	  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
741	  \[
742	    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
743	    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
744	  \]
745	  The identification of \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k}\) — the dual-number
746	  points over the identity section — with the
747	  \(\kappa(0)\)-linear dual of the cotangent space
748	  \(\mathfrak m_0/\mathfrak m_0^2\) is
749	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_cotangent_dual}.
750	
751	  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
752	  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
753	  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
754	  abelian groups
755	  \[
756	    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
757	        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
758	  \]
759	  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
760	  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
761	  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
762	  \[
763	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
764	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
765	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
766	  \]
767	
768	  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
769	  represents the étale-sheafification of
770	  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
771	  commutative diagram of abelian groups
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
775	        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
776	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
777	      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
778	        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
779	    \end{array}
780	  \]
781	  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
782	  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 2, Exercise~2.3;
783	  invocation legitimised by
784	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
785	  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
786	  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
787	  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
788	  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
789	  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
790	  detected after a faithfully flat extension).
791	
792	  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
793	  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
794	  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
795	  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
796	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
797	  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
798	  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.
799	
800	  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
801	  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
802	  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
803	\end{proof}

805	\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
806	\label{sec:pic0_smooth}
807	
808	By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
809	\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
810	equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
811	smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
812	at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
813	translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
814	dimension \(g\).

849	  \(g\): the structural morphism

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.TangentSpaceStalkAlgebra
7	
8	/-!
9	# Tangent-space substrate: rational sections and the identity section (A.3.iii substrate)
10	
11	The last generic layer of the Kleiman §5 tangent-space computation before it
12	lands on `Pic⁰_{C/k}`: a *section* of the structure morphism of a `k`-scheme
13	hits a `k`-rational point, so the tangent-space description
14	`overDualNumberAtEquivCotangentSpaceDual` of
15	`Picard/TangentSpaceStalkAlgebra.lean` applies at it unconditionally. For a
16	`k`-group scheme (an `Over (Spec k)`-object with a `MonObj`/`GrpObj`
17	instance) the unit `η : 𝟙_ ⟶ G` is such a section, giving the tangent space
18	at the identity.
19	
20	## Main declarations
21	
22	- `AlgebraicGeometry.stalkStructureHom_comp_stalkClosedPointTo` — a section
23	  `e : Spec k ⟶ X` of the structure morphism retracts the structure
24	  homomorphism `k ⟶ 𝒪_{X,e(*)}` via the induced stalk map `𝒪_{X,e(*)} ⟶ k`.
25	- `AlgebraicGeometry.bijective_algebraMap_residueField_of_section` — **the
26	  image of a section is a `k`-rational point**: `k → κ(e(*))` is bijective.
27	  Injectivity is automatic (field source); surjectivity follows from the
28	  retraction because a field homomorphism `κ(e(*)) → k` is injective.
29	- `AlgebraicGeometry.overDualNumberSectionEquivCotangentSpaceDual` — at the
30	  image of a section, the over-`Spec k` dual-number points of `X` form the
31	  `κ(x)`-linear dual of the cotangent space `m_x/m_x²`; no rationality
32	  hypothesis left.
33	- `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.identitySection` — the identity section
34	  `e : Spec k ⟶ G` of a `k`-monoid/group scheme, the `.left` of the unit
35	  `η[G] : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec k)) ⟶ G`; `identitySection_comp` says it is a
36	  section of `G.hom`.
37	- `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.identityDualNumberEquivCotangentSpaceDual`
38	  — **the tangent space of a `k`-group scheme at the identity**: over-`Spec k`
39	  dual-number points at the identity-section point are the `κ(e)`-linear dual
40	  of `m_e/m_e²`. This is the generic LHS of Kleiman §5 Thm. 5.11 for
41	  `G = Pic⁰_{C/k}`; the wiring to `Pic0Scheme C` lives in
42	  `Picard/Pic0AbelianVariety.lean` (`Pic0.tangentSpaceCotangentDual`), and
43	  only the `H¹(C, 𝒪_C)` identification of `thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso`
44	  remains (gated on the `AJC.picrep` cone).
45	
46	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex`,
47	§ `sec:pic0_tangent_space` (`lem:tangent_section_retraction`,
48	`lem:tangent_section_rational`, `cor:tangent_section_cotangent_dual`,
49	`def:tangent_identity_section`, `thm:tangent_identity_cotangent_dual`).
50	-/

53	set_option maxSynthPendingDepth 3

55	universe u
56	
57	open CategoryTheory IsLocalRing
58	
59	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
60	
61	section RationalSection
62	
63	variable {k : Type u} [Field k]
64	
65	/-- **A section retracts the structure homomorphism on stalks.** For a
66	scheme `X` over `Spec k` and a section `e : Spec k ⟶ X.left` of the
67	structure morphism, the stalk map of `e` at the closed point retracts the
68	structure homomorphism `k ⟶ 𝒪_{X, e(*)}`. Immediate from
69	`comp_eq_spec_iff` applied to `ψ = 𝟙 k`. -/
70	lemma stalkStructureHom_comp_stalkClosedPointTo
71	    (X : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) {e : Spec (CommRingCat.of k) ⟶ X.left}
72	    (he : e ≫ X.hom = 𝟙 (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
73	    stalkStructureHom X.hom (e.base (closedPoint k)) ≫ Scheme.stalkClosedPointTo e
74	      = 𝟙 (CommRingCat.of k) :=
75	  (comp_eq_spec_iff X.hom e (𝟙 (CommRingCat.of k))).mp (by rwa [Spec.map_id])
76	
77	/-- **The image of a section is a `k`-rational point.** For a scheme `X`
78	over `Spec k`, a section `e : Spec k ⟶ X.left` of the structure morphism and
79	`x` the image of the closed point under `e`, the structure map
80	`k → κ(x)` to the residue field is bijective. Injectivity is automatic
81	(`k` is a field); for surjectivity, the retraction
82	`stalkStructureHom_comp_stalkClosedPointTo` descends to a field
83	homomorphism `φ : κ(x) → k` splitting `k → κ(x)`, and a splitting by an
84	injective map is forced to be an inverse: `a = algebraMap k κ(x) (φ a)`
85	because `φ` kills the difference. -/
86	theorem bijective_algebraMap_residueField_of_section
87	    (X : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) {e : Spec (CommRingCat.of k) ⟶ X.left}
88	    (he : e ≫ X.hom = 𝟙 (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) {x : X.left}
89	    (hx : e.base (closedPoint k) = x) :
90	    Function.Bijective
91	      (algebraMap k (ResidueField (X.left.presheaf.stalk x))) := by
92	  subst hx
93	  have hretr := stalkStructureHom_comp_stalkClosedPointTo X he
94	  have hcomp : ∀ c : k,
95	      (Scheme.stalkClosedPointTo e).hom
96	        ((stalkStructureHom X.hom (e.base (closedPoint k))).hom c) = c := fun c => by
97	    simpa using DFunLike.congr_fun (congrArg CommRingCat.Hom.hom hretr) c
98	  set φ := IsLocalRing.ResidueField.lift (Scheme.stalkClosedPointTo e).hom with hφdef
99	  have hφ : ∀ c : k,
100	      φ (algebraMap k
101	        (ResidueField (X.left.presheaf.stalk (e.base (closedPoint k)))) c) = c := by
102	    intro c
103	    rw [← residue_algebraMap, hφdef, IsLocalRing.ResidueField.lift_residue_apply]
104	    exact hcomp c
105	  constructor
106	  · exact (algebraMap k
107	      (ResidueField (X.left.presheaf.stalk (e.base (closedPoint k))))).injective
108	  · intro a
109	    refine ⟨φ a, ?_⟩
110	    have h0 : φ (algebraMap k
111	        (ResidueField (X.left.presheaf.stalk (e.base (closedPoint k)))) (φ a) - a)
112	          = 0 := by
113	      rw [map_sub, hφ, sub_self]
114	    exact sub_eq_zero.mp (φ.injective (h0.trans (map_zero φ).symm))
115	
116	open TrivSqZeroExt in
117	/-- **The tangent space at the image of a section.** For a scheme `X` over
118	`Spec k` and a section `e` of the structure morphism, the over-`Spec k`
119	dual-number points of `X` at `x = e(*)` form the `κ(x)`-linear dual of the
120	cotangent space `m_x/m_x²` — `overDualNumberAtEquivCotangentSpaceDual` with
121	the rationality hypothesis discharged by
122	`bijective_algebraMap_residueField_of_section`. -/
123	noncomputable def overDualNumberSectionEquivCotangentSpaceDual
124	    (X : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) {e : Spec (CommRingCat.of k) ⟶ X.left}
125	    (he : e ≫ X.hom = 𝟙 (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) {x : X.left}
126	    (hx : e.base (closedPoint k) = x) :
127	    {g : Spec (CommRingCat.of (DualNumber k)) ⟶ X.left //
128	        g ≫ X.hom = Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap k (DualNumber k)))
129	          ∧ g.base (closedPoint (DualNumber k)) = x} ≃
130	      Module.Dual (ResidueField (X.left.presheaf.stalk x))
131	        (CotangentSpace (X.left.presheaf.stalk x)) :=
132	  overDualNumberAtEquivCotangentSpaceDual X x
133	    (bijective_algebraMap_residueField_of_section X he hx)
134	
135	end RationalSection
136	
137	namespace GroupScheme
138	
139	variable {k : Type u} [Field k]
140	
141	/-- **The identity section of a `k`-monoid/group scheme.** For an
142	`Over (Spec k)`-object `G` with a monoid-object structure, the underlying
143	scheme morphism `Spec k ⟶ G.left` of the unit `η[G] : 𝟙_ ⟶ G`. (For a group
144	scheme, `[GrpObj G]` supplies the `MonObj G` instance.) -/
145	noncomputable def identitySection (G : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) [MonObj G] :
146	    Spec (CommRingCat.of k) ⟶ G.left :=
147	  (MonObj.one (X := G)).left
148	
149	/-- The identity section is a section of the structure morphism: the unit is
150	a morphism in `Over (Spec k)` out of the monoidal unit `Over.mk (𝟙 (Spec k))`. -/
151	lemma identitySection_comp (G : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) [MonObj G] :
152	    identitySection G ≫ G.hom = 𝟙 (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) :=
153	  Over.w (MonObj.one (X := G))
154	
155	open TrivSqZeroExt in
156	/-- **The tangent space of a `k`-group scheme at the identity** (Kleiman §5
157	Thm. 5.11, LHS, generic form): for a `k`-monoid/group scheme `G`, the
158	over-`Spec k` dual-number points of `G` at the identity-section point
159	`e(*)` form the `κ(e(*))`-linear dual of the cotangent space
160	`m_{e(*)}/m_{e(*)}²`. No rationality hypothesis: the identity section makes
161	`e(*)` automatically `k`-rational. -/
162	noncomputable def identityDualNumberEquivCotangentSpaceDual
163	    (G : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) [MonObj G] :
164	    {g : Spec (CommRingCat.of (DualNumber k)) ⟶ G.left //
165	        g ≫ G.hom = Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap k (DualNumber k)))
166	          ∧ g.base (closedPoint (DualNumber k))
167	              = (identitySection G).base (closedPoint k)} ≃
168	      Module.Dual
169	        (ResidueField (G.left.presheaf.stalk ((identitySection G).base (closedPoint k))))
170	        (CotangentSpace (G.left.presheaf.stalk ((identitySection G).base (closedPoint k)))) :=
171	  overDualNumberSectionEquivCotangentSpaceDual G (identitySection_comp G) rfl
172	
173	end GroupScheme
174	
175	end AlgebraicGeometry

904	  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
905	  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
906	  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~5.23
907	  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
908	  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
909	  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
910	  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
911	  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~5.14): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
912	  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
913	  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
914	  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
915	  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
916	  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
917	  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
918	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
919	  above, it is smooth everywhere.
920	
921	  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
922	  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
923	\end{proof}

925	\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
926	\label{sec:pic0_proper}
927	
928	A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
929	so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
930	quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
931	projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
932	
933	\begin{theorem}\leanok
934	[Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
935	  \label{thm:pic0_proper}
936	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper}
937	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
938	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
939	
940	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p;
941	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
942	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2940).
943	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
944	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
945	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
946	  % and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
947	  % quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
948	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective.

953	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
954	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
955	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is projective over \(k\), in
956	  particular proper: the structural morphism
957	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is a projective morphism, hence

962	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
963	% L2941-L2984: the proof of th:qpp&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
964	% structure theorem to reduce projectivity to ruling out non-constant
965	% maps from the multiplicative group; the curve case relies on
966	% geometric normality of \(C\).)
967	% "Suppose \(X\) is also geometrically normal.  Since \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is
968	% quasi-projective, to prove it is projective, it suffices to prove it is
969	% proper.  By Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}, forming \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) commutes
970	% with extending \(k\).  And by \cite[2.7.1(vii)]{EGAIV2}, a \(k\)-scheme is
971	% complete if (and only if) it is after extending \(k\).  So we may, and do,
972	% assume \(k\) is algebraically closed.
973	%
974	% Recall the structure theorem of Chevalley and Rosenlicht for algebraic
975	% groups, or reduced connected group schemes of finite type over \(k\); see
976	% \cite[Thm.~1.1, p.~3]{Co02}.  The theorem says that every algebraic
977	% group is an extension of an Abelian variety (or complete algebraic
978	% group) by a linear (or affine) algebraic group.  Recall also that every
979	% solvable linear algebraic \(k\)-group is triangularizable (the
980	% Lie--Kolchin theorem); so, if it's nontrivial, then it contains a copy
981	% of the multiplicative group or of the additive group; see \cite[(10.5)
982	% and (10.2)]{Bo69}.  Now,  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is commutative, so solvable.
983	% Hence it suffices to show that, if \(T\) denotes the
984	% affine line minus the origin, then every \(k\)-map \(t\:T\to
985	% (\IPicz_{X/k})_{\red}\) is constant."
986	\begin{proof}
987	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
988	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
989	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
990	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~5.4, specialised to \(X = C\) a
991	  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
992	
993	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
994	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
995	  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
996	  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
997	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
998	  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
999	  (\S 5 Thm.~5.4, first conclusion) then gives that
1000	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
1001	  is projective and geometrically integral.
1002	
1003	  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
1004	  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
1005	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
1006	  commutes with extension of \(k\)
1007	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
1008	  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
1009	  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
1010	  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.
1011	
1012	  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
1013	  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
1014	  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
1015	  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
1016	  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
1017	  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
1018	  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
1019	  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
1020	  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
1021	  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
1022	  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
1023	  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
1024	  is constant.
1025	
1026	  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
1027	  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
1028	  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
1029	  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
1030	  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
1031	  theorem (Kleiman~\S 2 Thm.~2.5) \(t\) is represented by an
1032	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
1033	  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
1034	  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
1035	  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
1036	  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
1037	  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
1038	  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
1039	  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
1040	  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
1041	  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
1042	  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
1043	  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
1044	  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
1045	  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
1046	  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
1047	  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
1048	  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
1049	  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
1050	  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
1051	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
1052	  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
1053	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
1054	  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
1055	  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
1056	  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
1057	  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
1058	  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
1059	  properness.
1060	\end{proof}
1061	
1062	\section{Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
1063	\label{sec:pic0_geom_irred}
1064	
1065	Geometric irreducibility is delivered directly by the abstract
1066	identity-component substrate
1067	(\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}) applied
1068	to the \(k\)-group scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\). We restate the
1069	specialisation here for the assembly downstream.
1070	
1071	\begin{theorem}\leanok
1072	[Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
1073	  \label{thm:pic0_geom_irred}
1074	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible}
1075	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
1076	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
1077	
1078	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0;
1079	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
1080	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2921-L2929).
1081	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1082	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
1083	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
1084	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
1085	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
1086	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
1087	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
1088	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
1089	  % \(k\).
1090	  %  \end{prp}"
1091	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~5.3
1092	  (geometric irreducibility of the connected component of the
1093	  identity).}
1094	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1095	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
1096	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is geometrically irreducible: after
1097	  base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the scheme
1098	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) is irreducible.
1099	\end{theorem}
1100	
1101	\begin{proof}
1102	  \uses{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
1103	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes,
1104	        thm:fga_pic_representability}
1105	  Apply the abstract identity-component substrate to the \(k\)-group
1106	  scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), which is locally of finite type by
1107	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. By
1108	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}, the
1109	  identity component \((\Pic_{C/k})^0 = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite
1110	  type and geometrically irreducible over \(k\). The base-change
1111	  compatibility of
1112	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes} identifies
1113	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\),
1114	  closing the proof.
1115	\end{proof}
1116	
1117	\section{Assembly: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety}
1118	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
1119	
1120	A \(k\)-group scheme is an \emph{abelian variety} iff its underlying
1121	\(k\)-scheme is a complete (proper) connected group variety
1122	(Milne~\S I.1, p.~8). Combining the smoothness, properness, and
1123	geometric irreducibility statements of this chapter with the
1124	identity-component group-scheme structure of
1125	\cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} packages \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an
1126	abelian variety of dimension \(g\). This is the load-bearing A.3.vii
1127	gate for Route~A.
1128	
1129	\begin{theorem}\leanok
1130	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
1131	  \label{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
1132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety}
1133	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
1134	        thm:pic0_proper,
1135	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
1136	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
1137	
1138	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
1139	  % \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety) (read from
1140	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~14).
1141	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1142	  % "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian variety. As we
1143	  % shall see, they are projective, and (fortunately) commutative. Their
1144	  % group laws will be written additively. Thus \(t_a\) is now denoted
1145	  % \(x \mapsto x + a\) and \(e\) is usually denoted \(0\)."
1146	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
1147	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~51 (read from
1148	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3990-L3996).
1149	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1150	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
1151	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
1152	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
1153	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
1154	  % Exercise~\ref{ex:jac}."
1155	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
1156	  \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], ``The
1157	  Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~5.26 (the Jacobian
1158	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a
1159	  projective abelian \(S\)-scheme).}
1160	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
1161	  genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component
1162	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is an
1163	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)} of dimension \(g\): the underlying
1164	  \(k\)-scheme is smooth (\cref{thm:pic0_smooth}), proper
1165	  (\cref{thm:pic0_proper}), and geometrically irreducible
1166	  (\cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme
1167	  structure inherited from \(\Pic_{C/k}\) via the inclusion
1168	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
1169	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}). In
1170	  particular, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne~\S
1171	  I.1, Cor.~1.4).
1172	\end{theorem}
1173	
1174	\begin{proof}
1175	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
1176	        thm:pic0_proper,
1177	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
1178	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
1179	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
1180	  We assemble Milne's defining axioms of an abelian variety from the
1181	  four conjuncts established earlier in this chapter and in
1182	  \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}.
1183	
1184	  \emph{(i) Group-variety structure.} By
1185	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} applied to
1186	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
1187	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}), the identity component
1188	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
1189	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) compatible with the clopen inclusion.
1190	
1191	  \emph{(ii) Smoothness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}, the structural
1192	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth (of relative
1193	  dimension \(g\)). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth
1194	  \(k\)-variety: it is reduced (Milne's standing convention bundles
1195	  reducedness into ``variety''), geometrically reduced (by
1196	  smoothness over \(k\)), and locally of finite type.
1197	
1198	  \emph{(iii) Properness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_proper}, the structural
1199	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is proper (in fact
1200	  projective). This is Milne's ``complete'' axiom.
1201	
1202	  \emph{(iv) Connectedness (geometric irreducibility).} By
1203	  \cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}, the underlying scheme
1204	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; in particular it is
1205	  geometrically connected, hence connected.
1206	
1207	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, proper,
1208	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme equipped with a
1209	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure. By Milne~\S I.1, p.~8, ``a complete
1210	  connected group variety is called an abelian variety''. Thus
1211	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety over \(k\); commutativity is
1212	  automatic (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Its dimension is the relative
1213	  dimension of the smooth morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\),
1214	  which equals \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) by
1215	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso} and \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}.
1216	
1217	  This is the content of Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~5.26: for \(X/S\)
1218	  projective and smooth with connected curve fibres of genus
1219	  \(g > 0\) (specialise to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\)), the Jacobian
1220	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) exists and is a projective abelian
1221	  \(S\)-scheme. The genus-zero case is degenerate
1222	  (\(\Pic^0_{C/k} = 0\), Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)) but is
1223	  consistent with the assembly: a point scheme \(\Spec k\) is
1224	  trivially an abelian variety of dimension \(0\).
1225	\end{proof}

123	
124	/-- **Dual-number points of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` at the identity are the cotangent
125	dual** (Kleiman §5 Thm.~`thm:tgtsp`, LHS). Given the `k`-group-scheme
126	structure on `Pic0Scheme C` (supplied by
127	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism` once `Pic0Scheme`
128	unwinds to `IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)`), the identity section
129	`e : Spec k ⟶ Pic⁰_{C/k}` hits a `k`-rational point, and the over-`Spec k`
130	dual-number points of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` at `e` — the Zariski tangent space
131	`T₀ Pic⁰_{C/k}` in its functor-of-points form — form the `κ(e)`-linear dual
132	of the cotangent space `m_e/m_e²`.
133	
134	This is the geometric half of `tangentSpaceIso`; composing with the
135	`H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`-identification of `Dual (m_e/m_e²)` (gated on the `AJC.picrep`
136	representability cone) closes `thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso`. -/
137	theorem tangentSpaceCotangentDual {k : Type u} [Field k]
138	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
139	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
140	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom]
141	    (hgrp : Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))) :
142	    Nonempty (Σ' (e : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left),
143	      (e ≫ (Pic0Scheme C).hom = 𝟙 (Spec (.of k))) ×'
144	      ({g : Spec (CommRingCat.of (DualNumber k)) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left //
145	          g ≫ (Pic0Scheme C).hom
146	              = Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap k (DualNumber k)))
147	            ∧ g.base (IsLocalRing.closedPoint (DualNumber k)) = e.base default} ≃
148	        Module.Dual
149	          (IsLocalRing.ResidueField ((Pic0Scheme C).left.presheaf.stalk (e.base default)))
150	          (IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace
151	            ((Pic0Scheme C).left.presheaf.stalk (e.base default))))) := by
152	  obtain ⟨i⟩ := hgrp
153	  letI := i
154	  haveI : Subsingleton ↥(Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) :=
155	    inferInstanceAs (Subsingleton (PrimeSpectrum k))
156	  exact ⟨GroupScheme.identitySection (Pic0Scheme C),
157	    GroupScheme.identitySection_comp (Pic0Scheme C),
158	    overDualNumberSectionEquivCotangentSpaceDual (Pic0Scheme C)
159	      (GroupScheme.identitySection_comp (Pic0Scheme C))
160	      (congrArg _ (Subsingleton.elim _ _))⟩

162	/-- **Tangent space at the identity: `T₀ Pic⁰_{C/k} ≅ H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`.**
163	
164	The Kleiman §5 Thm.~`thm:tgtsp` tangent-space isomorphism. For a smooth
165	proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the Zariski tangent space at the
166	identity of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is canonically isomorphic to the first sheaf
167	cohomology `H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`.

169	In Lean (skeleton form): we bundle existentially over a `k`-rational
170	identity-section point `e : Spec k ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left` and a `k`-module
171	structure on the cotangent space `m_e/m_e²` (the natural `k`-module structure
172	arising from the `k`-algebra structure on the stalk at a `k`-rational point;

175	between the cotangent space and the project's `H¹(C, 𝒪_C)` module
176	`Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1`.

179	`k`-module structure on the cotangent space is consistently threaded through
180	downstream consumers; the underlying additive group structure is enough for
181	the dimension corollary `dim_k T₀ Pic⁰ = dim_k H¹ = g(C)`. -/
182	theorem tangentSpaceIso {k : Type u} [Field k]
183	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
184	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
185	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
186	    Nonempty (Σ' (e : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left),
187	      IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace ((Pic0Scheme C).left.presheaf.stalk (e.base default))
188	        ≃+ Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1) :=
189	  sorry

191	/-- **Smoothness of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.**
192	
193	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the identity component
194	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is smooth over `k` (Kleiman §5 Cor.~`cor:sm` + Cor.~`cor:ch0` in
195	characteristic zero + Ex.~`ex:jac` for the curve case).
196	
197	The proof (iter-194+): smoothness at the identity propagates to smoothness
198	everywhere by translation (Pic⁰ is a `k`-group scheme, so multiplication by
199	any `λ ∈ Pic⁰(k̄)` is an automorphism). At the identity, smoothness reduces
200	to the inequality
201	`dim_0 Pic⁰_{C/k} ≤ dim_k T₀ Pic⁰_{C/k} = dim_k H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = g(C)`
202	(`tangentSpaceIso`) becoming an equality. For a smooth proper curve this
203	equality holds: in characteristic zero by Cartier's theorem (every
204	`k`-group scheme is smooth), in positive characteristic by the curve-specific
205	vanishing `H²(C, 𝒪_C) = 0` (the obstruction to lifting tangent vectors). -/
206	theorem smooth {k : Type u} [Field k]
207	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
208	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
209	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
210	    Smooth (Pic0Scheme C).hom :=
211	  sorry
212	
213	/-- **Properness of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.**
214	
215	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the identity component
216	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is proper over `k` (Kleiman §5 Thm.~`th:qpp&p`; a smooth proper
217	curve is geometrically normal, hence the projectivity upgrade applies).
218	
219	The proof (iter-194+): `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is quasi-projective (Kleiman §5
220	Thm.~`th:qpp&p`, first conclusion); to upgrade to projective (hence proper)
221	we use the Chevalley–Rosenlicht structure theorem to reduce to ruling out
222	non-constant `k̄`-morphisms `𝔾_m → Pic⁰_{C/k̄}`. The latter follows from
223	normality of `C ×_k 𝔾_m` and Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15 (invertible sheaves on
224	a normal integral scheme are sheaves of Cartier divisors). -/
225	theorem proper {k : Type u} [Field k]
226	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
227	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
228	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
229	    IsProper (Pic0Scheme C).hom :=
230	  sorry

232	/-- **Geometric irreducibility of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.**
233	
234	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the identity component
235	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is geometrically irreducible over `k` (Kleiman §5 Prp.~`prp:pic0`,
236	specialisation of the abstract identity-component substrate
237	`IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible` of
238	`Picard/IdentityComponent.lean` to `G = PicScheme C`).
239

241	to `G = PicScheme C` (locally of finite type by `FGAPicRepresentability`), and
242	identify `IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)` with `Pic0Scheme C` via the
243	`Pic0Scheme` definition (sibling). -/

245	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
246	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
247	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
248	    GeometricallyIrreducible (Pic0Scheme C).hom :=
249	  sorry

251	/-- **`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety (A.3.vii assembly).**
252	
253	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the identity
254	component `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety over `k`: the underlying
255	`k`-scheme is smooth (`Pic0.smooth`), proper (`Pic0.proper`), and
256	geometrically irreducible (`Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible`), and it carries
257	a `k`-group-scheme structure inherited from `Pic_{C/k}` via the inclusion
258	`Pic⁰_{C/k} ↪ Pic_{C/k}`
259	(`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism`). Commutativity is
260	automatic (Milne §I.1, Cor.~1.4).
261	
262	Milne §I.1, p.~8: "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian
263	variety." This is the load-bearing A.3.vii gate of Route~A for
264	`thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness`.

267	`smooth` (this file), `geometricallyIrreducible` (this file), and the
268	`Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))` slot, the latter from
269	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism` applied to
270	`G = PicScheme C` (sibling `Picard/IdentityComponent.lean`). -/
271	theorem isAbelianVariety {k : Type u} [Field k]
272	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
273	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
274	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
275	    IsProper (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧ Smooth (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
276	      GeometricallyIrreducible (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
277	      Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C)) :=
